coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of silicon quantum dots-methyl viologen nanohybrids: Turn-On-Off-On fluorescence nanoprobe for the detection of d-penicillamine

coredata.dc:description: This study provides a straightforward, one-step hydrothermal approach for synthesizing water-soluble silicon quantum dots (SiQDs). The surface of the synthesized SiQDs is modified by the carboxylic acid functional group (A:SiQDs) after succinic anhydride treatment. The modified A:SiQDs, which emitted blue light fluorescence, showed high stability against photobleaching, thermal stability, and good water solubility. The A:SiQDs are conjugated with methyl viologen (MV2+) to develop a nanohybrid for specific D-Penicillamine (D-PA) detection with an LOD of 8 nM. The suggested sensor was effectively used to analyze D-PA in commercial pharmaceutical formulations using the conventional addition method, and it produced results with good accuracy and precision that were nearly equal to those measured.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study provides a straightforward, one-step hydrothermal approach for synthesizing water-soluble silicon quantum dots (SiQDs). The surface of the synthesized SiQDs is modified by the carboxylic acid functional group (A:SiQDs) after succinic anhydride treatment. The modified A:SiQDs, which emitted blue light fluorescence, showed high stability against photobleaching, thermal stability, and good water solubility. The A:SiQDs are conjugated with methyl viologen (MV2+) to develop a nanohybrid for specific D-Penicillamine (D-PA) detection with an LOD of 8 nM. The suggested sensor was effectively used to analyze D-PA in commercial pharmaceutical formulations using the conventional addition method, and it produced results with good accuracy and precision that were nearly equal to those measured.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Silicon quantum dot (SiQDs) is a promising fluorescent probe that is widely used for various biomedical applications, including bioimaging, sensing, target drug delivery, and therapeutic uses [1]; Haripriyan et al., 2022; [49]. In addition to their intrinsic electronic and optical properties, the nanostructured SiQDs have significant fluorescence with high brightness, size-dependent tunable light emission, high quantum efficiency, and photostability, which make them ideal fluorophore for imaging and sensing applications [43,49,59]. Hence, their usage as stable fluorophores is preferred over regular QDs for cellular images in several diagnostic assays. SiQDs are generally prone to areal oxidation, which can be solved by surface passivation with organic functional molecules through various chemical and photochemical reactions [4,44] and Chowdhury and Roy [10]. The organically functionalized SiQDs have shown some interesting additional properties like enhancing overall stability, increased solubility, preventing aggregation and precipitation in a biological environment. because of their excellent photoluminescence properties, robust chemical inertness, and low cytotoxicity [50,51]. SiQDs are considered a stable and cheaper fluorophore for biomedical and optoelectronic industries. Studies revealed that the sensitivity and selectivity of QDs based on emission could be improved by surface functionalization [21,37]. MV2+ is widely used as a herbicide and is classified in the viologen family, which has a structure similar to redox-active heterocycles [56,62,68]. MV2+ has two quaternary ammonium groups, which act as linkers between the individual metal QDs through electrostatic self-assembling and quench the metal QD's fluorescence by an efficient electron transfer process. Many researchers constructed MV2+ conjugated nanohybrids systems such as Mn-doped CdTe QDs-MV2+ and Mn-doped ZnSe QDs–MV2+ nanohybrids fluorescent probe for the detection of amino acids [17,27,56,62,68]. Since MV2+ is considered the prominent candidate for the detection of amino acids. Penicillamine (2-amino-3-mercapto-3-methylbutanoic acid) is a hydrolytic metabolite of penicillin and belongs to the aminothiols family and exists as D-and L- enantiomeric forms [6]. L-penicillamine (L-PA) is toxic and induces many adverse effects, such as neuritis and osteomyelitis [25].

On the other hand, D-PA is widely used to treat many diseases, particularly Wilson's disease, progressive systemic sclerosis, primary biliary fibrotic lung disease, rheumatoid arthritis, scleroderma, cystinuria, progressive systemic sclerosis, and an inhibitor for the replication of human immune deficiency virus [23]; Jones et al., 2005). Although it is beneficial for medicinal applications, a few after-effects have been reported in the literature due to the continuous intake of D-PA as a drug for various ailments [19,52]. Hence, developing rapid and sensitive strategies for quantitatively detecting D-PA at low concentrations is challenging. However, several detection protocols have been developed for detecting D-PA, such as HPLC [7], spectrofluorimetry [8,55], chemiluminescence [66], capillary electrophoreses [30,67], electrochemistry [60], potentiometric [38], flow cytometric analysis [15], and NMR spectroscopy [41].). Even though these methods have great selectivity and accuracy, they also have drawbacks, including expensive robotic equipment, challenging SOPs, time-consuming biological sample preparation, and limited detection limits [3,56,62]. Due to the ease of use, high sensitivity, specificity, and real-time determination, fluorescence assays are frequently preferred over other techniques.

Additionally, particularly in the fields of sensors and bioimaging, the most crucial tasks associated with the fluorescence-based sensor probe require producing sensitive and selective detection of specified analytes [32,34,47]. There has been a lot of interest in the selective and sensitive detection of D-PA-based fluorescent probes, such as organic dyes, fluorescent proteins, quantum dots, and noble-metal nanoclusters (NCs). Up till now, there has been a lot of interest in the selective and sensitive detection of D-PA-based fluorescent probes, such as organic dyes, fluorescent proteins, quantum dots, and noble-metal nanoclusters (NCs) [14,48,61]. The growing demand for biological and chemical applications is constrained by the fact that these fluorophores, despite their significant contribution to sensing D-PA, occasionally require time-consuming preparation, are toxic, have low sensitivity and selectivity, require expensive chemicals, and are insoluble in water (Krishnan et al., 2023; [39,42].

Herein, we synthesized and modified SiQDs, followed by generating a nanohybrid via conjugation with MV2+ (A:SiQDs@MV2+). The A:SiQDs@MV2+ emission intensity is quenched state due to the formation of nanohybrids. Further, fluorescence turn-on sensing can be applied to detect D-PA and the enhanced fluorescence intensity that occurs by the subsequent addition (Scheme 1 ). The proposed method is a robust, quick response of D-PA at a trace level with a low detection limit.

(3-Aminopropyl) trimethoxysilane (3-APTMS 97 %) and Methyl viologen hydrate was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (India), tri-sodium citrate (99 %) was purchased from Rankem chemicals, succinic anhydride (99 %), and Tetrahydrofuran (THF, 99.5 %) was received from SRL Pvt. Ltd., and dialysis membrane (MWCO 3000 Da) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (India), sodium hydroxide (98 %) and Conc. HCl (30 %) was used to adjust the pH of the solution. The stock solution of D-penicillamine was prepared in double distilled water, which was purchased from Sigma–Aldrich (India). Aaramine-250 capsules were purchased from local medicals (Chennai). Chemicals and solvents were purchased in analytical grade and used as received. Double-distilled (DD) water was used to make all the solutions throughout the experiment. All glass containers were cleaned by aqua-regia and rinsed with DD water.

Amine functionalized SiQDs were synthesized using a previously reported method with slight modification [50]. About 500 mg of sodium citrate and 0.5 mL of 3-APTMS were dissolved in 15 mL water. The solution was subsequently transferred into a Teflon tube that was equipped with a stainless-steel autoclave. Hydrothermal treatment was conducted at a temperature of 200 °C for 12 h. The autoclave was allowed to cool down to room temperature after the hydrothermal treatment. Finally, SiQDs are mixed with cold DD water, dialyzed for 24 h for purification, and kept in a darkroom for further experiments. The yield was calculated to be about 1.050 mg/mL by weighing the dried SiQDs-NH2.

To functionalize carboxylic acid over the surface of the SiQDs, the reaction mixture of synthesized SiQDs was reacted with succinic anhydride in a 1:1 M ratio [53]. Briefly, 1 g of SiQDs was dissolved in 50 mL of anhydrous tetrahydrofuran (THF), and the suspension was dispersed with ultra-sonication for 30 min. Then 3.5 g of succinic anhydride was added into two portions to the reaction medium, and the temperature was fixed at 0 °C. Then the reaction temperature was raised to 30 °C, allowing a further 24 h in the dark room. The A:SiQDs were dispersed by ultra-sonication for 15 min and centrifuged at 14000 rpm for 1 h. Subsequently, the precipitate was centrifuged with anhydrous THF, further dispersed in DD water, and then purified by centrifuge. The resulting precipitate had A:SiQDs, which were isolated and stored in an N2 atmosphere.

A:SiQDs@MV2+ nanohybrids were prepared by mixing 300 μL of SiQDs stock solution with different concentrations of MV2+ ranging from 10 nM to 200 nM into a glass vial. Subsequently, the sample was mixed thoroughly with DD water up to 3 mL. After the optimum incubation time interval, the fluorescence spectra were recorded. The fluorescence intensity of SiQDs gradually decreases upon successive addition of different concentrations of MV2+, which indicates the formation of A:SiQDs@MV2+ nanohybrids. A selectivity experiment was conducted using various amino acids as potential interferences instead of D-PA to assess selectivity. The experiment was performed under identical conditions, and the amino acids included glutamic acid (Glu), arginine (Arg), histidine (His), valine (Val), tyrosine (Tyr), tryptophan (Trp), proline (Pro), aspartic acid (Asp), and methionine (Met), cysteine (cys) and Glutathione (GSH). Moreover, 1 min of incubation time was given after mixing the sample with the system, and the fluorescence spectra of the above solutions were recorded.

The absorption spectra of SiQDs were measured using a Shimadzu UV-1601 spectrophotometer in the range of 180–1100 nm. The measurements were conducted with a double-beam configuration, and the test solutions were placed in quartz cuvettes with a path length of 1 cm. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy measurements were carried out using a Perkin Elmer spectrometer. A pellet was prepared by mixing the solid sample of SiQDs with KBr in a 1:100 ratio. The FT-IR spectra were recorded in the spectral range from 400 to 4000 cm−1. Background correction was performed by subtracting the spectrum of KBr. Fluorescence spectra of SiQDs were recorded using a spectrofluorometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, USA). A 450 W Xenon lamp was utilized as the light source. For fluorescence lifetime measurement, the time-correlated single-photon-counting (TCSPC) technique was employed using an IBH Model 5000U instrument from Glasgow, UK. The excitation of samples was achieved using a vertically polarized laser at 375 nm, generated by a flexible harmonic generator (Spectra-Physics, GWU 23PS). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were obtained using an FEI-Technai G2 MODEL T-30 S-TWIN microscope operating at an acceleration voltage of 250 kV. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) images were acquired using an FEI Quanta 3D microscope with a gun voltage of 10 kV. The SiQD samples were drop-casted onto Si-wafer (111) substrates. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were obtained from Omicron Nano Technology GmbH, Germany. Zeta potential measurements were performed using a Horiba SZ-100 instrument.

An aliquot of 100 µL of A:SiQDs was taken in a clean glass vial for the D-PA sensing. Each vial was then filled with 50 µL of methyl viologen at the specified concentration (1.0 × 10–5 mol/L) and incubated for 5 min. Following that, various D-PA concentrations were thoroughly mixed with nanohybrid in each vial, and the final solution was made up of double distilled water for 3 mL. The excitation was fixed at 350 nm and emission spectra were recorded ranging from 365 to 700 nm. The emission and excitation slits were 3 and 5 nm, respectively. All the experiments were carried out under identical conditions to optimize the detection limit and were repeated three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of binding affinity with MV2+
ce:section.ce:para:
The interaction of MV2+ with A:SiQDs was analyzed using the Stern Volmer equation (1).
(1)
F o
/ F = I + K s v
Q

F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity ratio (F0/F) of A:SiQDs in the absence and presence of MV2+, respectively. Ksv is the Stern–Volmer quenching rate constant, and [Q] is the quencher concentration of MV2+. The supplement of Fig. 4B, results shows the plot of the corresponding fluorescence intensity ratio (F0/F) of A:SiQDs versus the concentration of MV2+.

Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) images and elemental mapping confirm the dispersed arrangement of A:SiQDs, with a notable abundance of Si. These observations provide evidence for the formation of quantum dots, as shown in Fig. 1 . TEM analysis with different magnifications confirms the morphology of as-synthesized A:SiQDs. The obtained images demonstrate the monodispersity and spherical shape of A:SiQDs. The size distribution of the particles aligns well with a Gaussian peak, indicating an average size of approximately 4.7 nm. This is illustrated in Fig. 2 A and B, respectively. The inset in the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) image reveals the high crystallinity of A:SiQDs, with discernible lattice fringes exhibiting an interplanar spacing of 0.30 nm, corresponding to the (111) plane of diamond Si [20,24,59]. Furthermore, Fourier transforms infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was employed to examine the functionalization of amine (NH2) and carboxylic acid (COOH) groups on the surface of SiQDs. The FT-IR spectrum, ranging from 4000 to 400 cm−1, is presented in Fig. 2C. The O-H bending mode and Si-O-Si asymmetric stretching are confirmed by the appearance of peaks at 1399 and 1033 cm−1, respectively. The vibration bands appeared at 3456 and 2931 cm−1, corresponding to N-H stretching frequency and C-H stretching vibrations, respectively, where the peak is suppressed, indicating functionalization.

Furthermore, the new vibrational bands that appeared at 3407 and 1630 cm−1 confirm the existence of O − H and C = O groups on the surface of A:SiQDs [2,9,13,54]. Furthermore, the elemental composition of the as-synthesized A:SiQDs was investigated using X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS analysis revealed specific peaks at energy levels of 101.6, 152.6, 284.6, 399.6, and 531.6 eV, corresponding to Si 2p, Si 2 s, C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively (Fig. 2D). In the C 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2E), four distinct peaks were observed at 283.6 eV (C-Si), 284.3 eV (C-C/C = C), 285.2 eV (C-N), and 288.5 eV (C-O). The N 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2F) exhibited three fitting peaks at 398.7 eV (N-C), 399.6 eV (Si-N-O), and 397.7 eV (Si-N-Si). The O 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2G) displayed two peaks at 532.2 and 535.2 eV for O-C, and two peaks at 530.3 and 531.1 eV representing O-Si. Finally, the Si 2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 2H) indicated peaks at 100.2, 101.2, and 102.3 eV, corresponding to Si-C, Si-N, and Si-O, respectively. These XPS results were consistent with the findings obtained from Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) analysis [29,35,43,59]. Moreover, a zeta potential measurement was carried out to confirm the ability of the surface charge and stability of SiQDs and A:SiQDs [11,36]. The obtained value was 12.2 mV and −27 mV, respectively (Fig. S1). Because of the negative charge on the surface, the prepared QDs exhibit high stability in the aqueous medium.

The optical properties of A:SiQDs were assessed through UV–visible and fluorescence spectroscopy. The absorption spectra of A:SiQDs exhibited a shoulder peak around 350 nm. When excited at 350 nm, the solution of A:SiQDs displayed a strong emission peak at 430 nm, indicating its blue emission properties. The emission peak had a full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 80 nm, as depicted in Fig. 3 A. Furthermore, the excitation-dependent emission properties of A:SiQDs were studied in an aqueous medium upon varying the excitation wavelength with a 10 nm increment from 330 to 400 nm. The intense emission peak was observed upon exciting at 350 nm, as shown in Fig. 3B. This excitation wavelength dependency may be due either to the diversity in the particle size of QDs or to the diverse surface functional group defects at the surface of the QDs.

The stability of A:SiQDs was optimized by changing the variables such as photostability, temperature, and pH in the aqueous medium. The fluorescent intensity of A:SiQDs does not show significant changes upon continuous UV light irradiation at 365 nm for 5 h, showing excellent photostability (Fig. 3C). Similarly, the effect of thermal stability of A:SiQDs was demonstrated at different temperatures ranging from 20 to 45 °C, and the changes in fluorescence intensity were monitored ( Fig. 3D). The A:SiQDs were dispersed in the desired pH of a buffer solution ranging from 2 to 12 and the fluorescence intensity changes were monitored. With the increase of the pH window from 2 to 12, the fluorescence intensity of A:SiQDs first increased and remained stable in physiological conditions, followed by a decrease in basic conditions (Fig. 3E). The above-mentioned results indicated that the prepared A:SiQDs possessed pH stability at a neutral state, good thermal stability, and photostability. The relative quantum yield was calculated as 13.3 % (λex = 350 nm) using the standard reference as quinine sulfate. Based on the fluorescence decay curve, the lifetime of A:SiQDs was 4.0 ns with triple-exponential behavior (Fig. 3F), and all fitting parameters are given in Table 1 .

MV2+ is a strong electron transfer agent, and while conjugated as nanohybrids with A:SiQDs, the fluorescence is in the Off state due to an efficient electron transfer process [30,67]. As a result, upon addition of MV2+ results in the quenching of emission intensity of A:SiQDs. Fig. 4 A demonstrates the gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity of A:SiQDs with the increasing concentration of MV2+ (200 nM). Herein, 200 nM of MV2+ was chosen as the optimum concentration for constructing nanohybrids and utilized for further study. A:SiQDs@MV2+ nanohybrids formed through electrostatic interaction of negatively charged carboxylate group of A:SiQDs with positively charged MV2+. Accordingly, the emission properties of A:SiQDs disappeared while increasing the concentration of MV2+ under UV light irradiation at 365 nm (inset of Fig. 4A). The binding affinity of MV2+ with A:SiQDs was analyzed using the Stern-Volmer equation [5,26,28]. The fluorescence intensity ratio linearly increases with respect to the increase in the concentration of MV2+ ranging from 10 to 70 nM, and the KSV value was found to be 7.05 × 106 M−1 (Fig. 4B). The linear regression coefficient for the analysis was determined to be R2 = 0.98. The limit of detection (LOD) was determined to be 2.76 nM using the formula LOD = 3 s/k. In this equation, s denotes the standard deviation of the blank solution, while k represents the slope of the calibration curve. However, the plot deviates from linearity at higher concentration ranges, indicating that quenching is occurring to a significant extent. The fluorescence was in the turn-Off state because electrons from A:SiQDs surface traps were successfully transported to the MV2+ acceptors. Subsequently, the fluorescence intensity was reinstated (turn-ON state) after D-PA was added as indicated to the solution [4,46,58,63]. The interference effect of some amino acids was investigated in different concentration ranges (Fig. 3G). This plot shows that D-PA can selectively respond with the nanohybrids and other amino acids. Even at high concentrations, it does not influence the intensity of A:SiQDs. Hence, we preferred to use MV2+ to prepare nanohybrids for our turn-On-Off-On fluorescence probe studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of D-PA using A:SiQDs@MV2+ nanohybrids
ce:section.ce:para: A sensitive turn-Off-On fluorescent probe strategy for D-PA assay was carried out based on the affinity towards MV2+. In order to evaluate the restoring ability of fluorescence intensity of A:SiQDs, the interaction between A:SiQDs@MV2+ nanohybrids and D-PA under identical conditions was demonstrated. Fig. 4C shows that the presence of D-PA significantly improves the emission characteristics of A:SiQDs@MV2+ nanohybrids at 430 nm, proving that D-PA can function as a fluorescence-restoring reagent with the A:SiQDs@MV2+ nanohybrids. Hence, it could be used as a highly selective turn-Off-On sensing probe to detect D-PA. The fluorescence intensity linearly increased with the concentration of D-PA up to 120 nM (Fig. 4D). The linear regression coefficient was R2 = 0.98, with a LOD of 8 nM corresponding to the linear range of 10 to 60 nM. However, above this concentration, no further variation in the fluorescence intensity was noted, indicating that the D-PA molecules were electrostatically self-assembled over the surface of A:SiQDs@MV2+ nanohybrids and the fluorescence intensity is restored up to 58 %. The proposed sensor shows excellent sensitivity for detecting D-PA in the presence of A:SiQDs@MV2+ nanohybrids with a low limit of detection values obtained compared to the other methods (Table 2 )Lakowicz and [28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for the recognition of D-PA
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme S2 shows the quenching mechanism of MV2+ on A:SiQDs. An electron will transition from the valence band into the conduction band of SiQDs upon exposure to light. When the light emanates. the excited electrons return to the valence band with ease. But when MV2+ is present, the electron travels along the bond pathway from the conduction band to MV2+, quenching the fluorescence since MV2+ is a superior electron acceptor. The results from this study prove that the close proximity of A:SiQDs caused by MV2+ chelation with the carboxylate and hydroxyl group of A:SiQDs is responsible for the MV2+-induced fluorescence quenching of A:SiQDs. This impacts the electronic structure of A:SiQDs and influences the exciton distribution, thereby promoting the non-radiative recombination of excitons via the charge transfer process and leading to the fluorescence quenching of A:SiQDs. One important characteristic of D-PA is its thiol group, which forms an MV2+-S bond and gives D-PA a significant binding preference towards MV2+. The competitive interaction between MV2+ and D-PA. The A:SiQDs is disrupted by the presence of D-PA, which affects the interaction between MV2+ and A:SiQDs. The system's fluorescence could then be restored once MV2+ was eliminated from the surface of A:SiQDs. It is feasible to use A:SiQDs for D-PA detection because of their distinct fluorescence responses when MV2+ and D-PA are added.

To verify the developed fluorescent probe's ability to detect D-PA in commercial pharmaceutical formulations, an analysis was conducted using the standard addition method and the recoveries. Table 3 presents the results. The range of D-PA recoveries for individual samples was 105.2 % to 106.2 %. The three samples' relative standard deviations (RSD) ranged from 0.18 % to 0.22 %. Real samples excipients don't obstruct the determination. Consequently, it is clear that the A:SiQDs@MV2+ nanohybrids can be effectively used to measure D-PA in real pharmaceutical samples.

In the present investigation, we synthesized blue-emitting SiQDs using a one-pot hydrothermal condition. The synthesized SiQDs were further surface modified with carboxylic acid by treating with succinic anhydride. The morphology of as-prepared A:SiQDs were ∼ 4.7 nm sized mono-dispersed with a spherical shape. Finally, A:SiQDs@MV2+ nanohybrids were prepared by mixing A:SiQDs with MV2+ through coulomb interaction. The prepared nanohybrids were employed to detect the concentration of the D-PA-based turn-On-Off-On nanoprobe. This design approach can be used to detect the d-PA in real samples with good selectivity and sensitivity, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 8 nM. With good accuracy and precision, the suggested sensor was used to analyze d-PA in commercial pharmaceutical formulations using the conventional addition method, and the results showed levels that were nearly equal to those measured.
